volumeTitle: Amphitryon. The Comedy of Asses. The Pot of Gold. The Two Bacchises. The Captives
label: The Pot of Gold (Section 2.1.151 - Section 2.2.220)

sections.label: Section 2.1.151
sections.original: Quia míhi misero cerebrum éxcutiunt
sections.translation: Because , sister , your words are knocking out the brains of unfortunate me ; you are speaking stones [*] .

sections.label: Section 2.1.153
sections.original: Heia , hoc face quod te iubet soror .
sections.translation: Well , well , do this that your sister requests you .

sections.label: Section 2.1.153b
sections.original: Si lubeat , faciam .
sections.translation: If she requests me , I will do it .

sections.label: Section 2.1.154
sections.original: In rem hoc tuam est .
sections.translation: ’ Tis for your own interest .

sections.label: Section 2.1.154b
sections.original: Vt quidem emoriar prius quam ducam .
sections.translation: Yes , for me to die before I marry .

sections.label: Section 2.1.155
sections.original: sed hís legibús si quam dáre vis dúcam :
sections.translation: Let her who comes here to-morrow , be carried out [*] of the house the day after , sister ; on that condition , give me her whom you wish to give ; get ready the nuptials .

sections.label: Section 2.1.158
sections.original: Cum máxima possúm tibi , fratér , dare dote ;
sections.translation: I am able , brother , to provide you with a wife with a very large marriage-portion .

sections.label: Section 2.1.160
sections.original: eam sí iubes , fratér , tibi me póscere , poscam .
sections.translation: But she ’ s somewhat aged ; she ’ s of the middle-age of woman . If you request me , brother , to ask her for you , I ’ ll ask her .

sections.label: Section 2.1.161
sections.original: Núm non vis me intérrogare te ?
sections.translation: Would you like me to ask you a question ?

sections.label: Section 2.1.161b
sections.original: Ímmo , si quid vís , roga .
sections.translation: Yes , if you like , ask it .

sections.label: Section 2.1.162
sections.original: Post mediam aetatem qui media ducit uxorem domum ,
sections.translation: Suppose any old man , past mid-age , brings home a middle-aged wife , if by chance he should have a child by this old woman , do you doubt at all but that the name of that child is Posthumus [*] , all prepared ?

sections.label: Section 2.1.165
sections.original: nunc ego istúm , soror , laborem demam et deminuam tibi .
sections.translation: Now , sister , I ’ ll remove and lessen this labour for you . I , by the merits of the Gods and of my forefathers , am rich enough ; these high families , haughty pride [*] , bountiful portions , acclamations , imperiousness , vehicles inlaid with ivory , superb mantles and purple , I can ’ t abide , things that by their extravagance reduce men to slavery .

sections.label: Section 2.1.170
sections.original: Dic mihi , quaeso , quis ea est , quam vis ducere uxorem ?
sections.translation: Tell me , pray , who is she whom you would like to take for a wife ?

sections.label: Section 2.1.170b
sections.original: Eloquar .
sections.translation: I ’ ll tell you . Do you know that Euclio , the poor old man close by ?

sections.label: Section 2.1.172
sections.original: Novi , hominem haud malum mecastor .
sections.translation: I know him ; not a bad sort of man , i ’ faith .

sections.label: Line 172b
sections.original: Eius cupio filiam
sections.translation: I ’ d like his maiden daughter to be promised me in marriage . Don ’ t make any words [*] about it , sister ; I know what you are going to say ; that she ’ s poor . This poor girl pleases me .

sections.label: Section 2.1.175
sections.original: Di bene vortant .
sections.translation: May the Gods prosper it .

sections.label: Section 2.1.175b
sections.original: Idem ego spero .
sections.translation: I hope the same .

sections.label: Section 2.1.175c
sections.original: Quid me ? num quid vis ?
sections.translation: What do you want me now for ? Do you wish for anything ?

sections.label: Section 2.1.175d
sections.original: Vale .
sections.translation: Farewell .

sections.label: Section 2.1.176
sections.original: Et tu , frater .—
sections.translation: And you the same , brother .

sections.label: Section 2.1.176b
sections.original: Ego conveniam Eúclionem , si domi est .
sections.translation: I ’ ll go meet Euclio , if he ’ s at home . But , see ! the very person is betaking himself home , whence , I know not .

sections.label: Section 2.2.178
sections.original: Praesagibat mi animus frustra me ire , quom exibam domo ;
sections.translation: My mind had a presentiment that I was going to no purpose when I left my house ; and therefore I went unwillingly ; for neither did any one of the wardsmen come ,

sections.label: Section 2.2.180
sections.original: venit neque magister quem dividere argentum oportuit .
sections.translation: nor yet the master of the ward , who ought to have distributed the money . Now I ’ m making all haste to hasten home ; for I myself am here , my mind ’ s at home .

sections.label: Section 2.2.182
sections.original: Salvos atque fortunatus , Euclio , semper sies .
sections.translation: May you be well , and ever fortunate , Euclio !

sections.label: Section 2.2.183
sections.original: Di te ament , Megadore .
sections.translation: May the Gods bless you , Megadorus !

sections.label: Section 2.2.183b
sections.original: Quid tu ? recten atque ut vis vales ?
sections.translation: How are you ? Are you quite well , and as you wish ?

sections.label: Section 2.2.184
sections.original: Non temerarium est , ubi dives blande appellat pauperem .
sections.translation: It isn ’ t for nothing when a rich man accosts a poor man courteously ;

sections.label: Section 2.2.185
sections.original: iam illic homo aurum scit me habere , eo me salutat blandius .
sections.translation: now this fellow knows that I ’ ve got some gold ; for that reason he salutes me more courteously .

sections.label: Section 2.2.186
sections.original: Ain tu te valere ?
sections.translation: Do you say that you are well ?

sections.label: Section 2.2.186b
sections.original: Pol ego haud perbene a pecunia .
sections.translation: Troth , I ’ m not very well in the money line .

sections.label: Section 2.2.187
sections.original: Pol si est animus aequos tibi , sat hábes qui bene vitam colas .
sections.translation: I ’ faith , if you ’ ve a contented mind , you have enough to passing a good life with .

sections.label: Section 2.2.188
sections.original: Anus hercle huic indicium fecit de auro , perspicue palam est ,
sections.translation: By my faith , the old woman has made a discovery to him about the gold ; ’ tis clear it ’ s all out . I ’ ll cut off her tongue , and tear out her eyes , when I get home .

sections.label: Section 2.2.190
sections.original: Quid tu solus tecum loquere ?
sections.translation: Why are you talking to yourself ?

sections.label: Section 2.2.190b
sections.original: Meam pauperiem conqueror .
sections.translation: I ’ m lamenting my poverty ; I ’ ve a grown-up girl without a portion , and one that can ’ t be disposed of in marriage ; nor have I the ability to marry her to anybody .

sections.label: Section 2.2.192b
sections.original: Tace , bonum habe animum , Euclio .
sections.translation: Hold your peace ; be of good courage , Euclio : she shall be given in marriaye ; you shall be assisted by myself . Say , if you have need of aught ; command me .

sections.label: Section 2.2.194
sections.original: Nunc petit , cum pollicetur ; inhiat aurum ut devoret .
sections.translation: Now is he aiming at my property , while he ’ s making promises ; he ’ s gaping for my gold , that he may devour it ;

sections.label: Section 2.2.195
sections.original: altera manu fert lapidem , panem ostentat altera .
sections.translation: in the one hand he is carrying a stone [*] . while he shows the bread in the other . I trust no person , who , rich himself , is exceedingly courteous to a poor man ; when he extends his hand with a kind air , then is he loading you with some damage . I know these polypi [*] , who , when they ’ ve touched a thing , hold it fast .

sections.label: Section 2.2.199
sections.original: Da mi operam parumper . paucis , Euclio , est quod te volo
sections.translation: Give me your attention , Euclio , for a little time : I wish to address you in a few words ,

sections.label: Section 2.2.200
sections.original: de communi re appellare mea et tua .
sections.translation: about a common concern of yours and mine .

sections.label: Section 2.2.200b
sections.original: Ei misero mihi ,
sections.translation: Alas ! woe is me ! my gold has been grabbed from in-doors : now he ’ s wishing for this thing , I ’ m sure , to come to a compromise with me ; but I ’ ll go look in my house .

sections.label: Section 2.2.203
sections.original: Quo abis ?
sections.translation: Where are you going ?

sections.label: Section 2.2.203b
sections.original: Iam ad te revortar : nunc est quod visam domum .—
sections.translation: I ’ ll return to you directly , for there ’ s something I must go and see to at home .

sections.label: Section 2.2.204
sections.original: Credo edepol , ubi mentionem ego fecero de filia ,
sections.translation: By my troth , I do believe that when I make mention of his daughter ,

sections.label: Section 2.2.205
sections.original: mi ut despondeat , sese a me derideri rebitur ;
sections.translation: for him to promise her to me , he ’ ll suppose that he ’ s being laughed at by me ; nor is there out of the whole class of paupers one more beggarly than he .

sections.label: Section 2.2.207
sections.original: Di me servant , salva res est . salvom est si quid non perit .
sections.translation: The Gods do favour me ; my property ’ s all safe . If nothing ’ s lost , it ’ s safe . I was very dreadfully afraid , before I went in-doors ! I was almost dead ! ( Aloud .) I ’ m come back to you , Megadorus , if you wish to say anything tome .

sections.label: Section 2.2.209b
sections.original: Habeo gratiam .
sections.translation: I return you thanks ; I beg that as to what I shall enquire of you , you ’ ll not hesitate to speak out boldly .

sections.label: Section 2.2.211
sections.original: Dum quidem né quid perconteris quod non lubeat proloqui .
sections.translation: So long , indeed , as you enquire nothing that I mayn ’ t choose to speak out upon .

sections.label: Section 2.2.212
sections.original: Dic mihi , quali me arbitrare genere prognatum ?
sections.translation: Tell me , of what sort of family do you consider me to be sprung ?

sections.label: Section 2.2.212b
sections.original: Bono .
sections.translation: Of a good one .

sections.label: Section 2.2.213
sections.original: Quid fide ?
sections.translation: What think you as to my character ?

sections.label: Section 2.2.213b
sections.original: Bona .
sections.translation: ’ Tis a good one .

sections.label: Section 2.2.213c
sections.original: Quid factis ?
sections.translation: What of my conduct ?

sections.label: Section 2.2.213d
sections.original: Neque malis neque improbis .
sections.translation: Neither bad nor dishonest .

sections.label: Section 2.2.214
sections.original: Aetatem meam scís ?
sections.translation: Do you know my years ?

sections.label: Section 2.2.214b
sections.original: Scio esse grandem , item ut pecuniam .
sections.translation: I know that they are plentiful , just like your money .

sections.label: Section 2.2.215
sections.original: Certe edepol equidem te civem sine mala omni malitia
sections.translation: I ’ faith , for sure I really did always take you to be a citizen without any evil guile , and now I think you so .

sections.label: Section 2.2.216b
sections.original: Aurum huic olet .
sections.translation: He smells the gold . ( Aloud .) What do you want with me now ?

sections.label: Section 2.2.217b
sections.original: Quoniam tu me et ego te qualis sis scio ,
sections.translation: Since you know me , and I know you , what sort of person you are — a thing , that may it bring a blessing on myself , and you and your daughter , I ask your daughter as my wife . Promise me that it shall be so .

sections.label: Section 2.2.220
sections.original: Heia , Megadore , haud decorum facinus tuis factis facis ,
sections.translation: Heyday ! Megadorus , you are doing a deed that ’ s not becoming to your usual actions , in laughing at me , a poor man , and guiltless towards yourself and towards your family . For neither in act , nor in words , have I ever deserved it of you , that you should do what you are now doing .
